text: The surgeon trims the right ovary, revealing paper-thin cortical tissue with no visible follicles. The lining is carefully pulled out and removed.

The surgeon peels away the thin cortical tissue of the ovary, resembling the layers of an onion, revealing minimal follicles. The lining of a larger cyst is stripped off, exposing another cyst filled with chocolate-colored material, which is opened and drained. Another cyst is peeled off and removed.

The surgeon examines the left ovary, revealing multiple follicles involved due to direct invasion from the right ovary. The ovary exhibits features typical of polycystic ovarian condition, with multiloculated cysts visible. Traction and counter-traction are applied to remove the cyst lining, revealing chocolate material and larger cysts. The remaining ovarian tissue appears abnormal with reduced volume, and several particles are visible at the bottom.

The surgeon examines the pelvic cavity, focusing on the ovaries which have been closed and cleaned. A subserosal broad-based myoma is identified, and the surgeon carefully pulls it out, noting that its capsule was invaded by endometrioma.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to remove a myoma from a wide surgical bed, noting some oozing but no significant bleeding. The vascular pedicle is trimmed, and the base is closed with interrupted extracorporeal sutures. The pelvic cavity is examined, revealing deep lesions in both parametral areas. A rectal probe is in place, and the posterior urinary wall is closed for hemostasis. The plan includes resecting deep lesions from the peritoneal side, with careful dissection around the ureters and urinary artery due to their proximity to the lesions.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the ureter, ensuring its direction is under direct observation. The dissection proceeds under the peritoneum to assess the depth of the lesion while avoiding excessive tissue removal. The hypogastric nerve is not dissected, and the deep lesion is isolated for removal. The presence of fatty tissue indicates clear margins.

The surgeon identifies a large peritoneal defect on the right side, suspecting cystic endometriosis. The ureter is exposed and identified, with careful dissection to avoid the hypogastric nerve and its branches. The perimetrium is pulled from the pelvic floor, revealing a cystic lesion approximately three-fourths of a centimeter in size. The cyst is excised to prevent progression of the endometriotic lesion. The pelvic floor on the right side is cleared, and remaining deep lesions on the left paramecium are removed, resulting in a clean pelvic cavity.

The surgeon closes the posterior cul-de-sac and peritonealizes the area to prevent small bowel adhesion and obstruction. The ovaries are opened, revealing a peritoneal defect in the anterior cul-de-sac on the right side. A cystic lesion is identified and resected with clear margins. The defective peritoneum is closed with a pursing suture, and the surface lesion is removed. Specimens are sent to pathology for confirmation of endometriosis.
